text: Living in the nightmarish hellscape that
is modern entertainment for the past
decade or so, you could almost forget
that there was once a time when all this
stuff was just beginning. When the idea
of terrible reboots that desecrated the
originals, shameless cash grab sequels
and agendadriven storytelling was still
a novel curiosity to be laughed at and
ridiculed. Oh, how naive we were. Well,
just like a global plague that goes on
to ravage the entire human race, it all
had to start somewhere. And these days,
most people seem to agree that patient
zero for all of this was Ghostbusters
2016 or Paul Feige's Ghostbusters or
Ghostbusters Answer the Call or An
Evening at the Improv featuring the
Ghostbusters. Whatever. You all know
what I'm talking about here. When it
comes to infamous movies that never
should have been made, they don't come
much more derided and despised than this
turd of a film. Born from a terrible
idea by a studio desperate to milk its
legacy franchises, written and directed
by the worst possible man for the job.
Starring a cast of SNL losers that were
about as funny as terminal cancer, and
somehow managing to insert itself into
the middle of the most divisive and
politically charged moment in American
culture for decades. It turned out to be
the prototype for everything wrong with
modern Hollywood, and it pretty much set
the tone for a new dark age of movie
making. But the actual story of how it
came into existence is almost as
disastrous as the film itself. So grab
yourself a pint of ectoplasm and join me
as I tell you the tale of Ghostbusters
2016. Our story begins way back in 1989
with the release of Ghostbusters 2. Most
of the cast weren't keen to do a sequel,
but Colombia Pictures were adamant. They
wanted another big hit like the 1984
original and eventually strongarmed
Harold Ramos and Dan Akroyd into writing
a new script. Ghostbusters 2 ended up
performing well enough at the box
office, but reviews were mixed. The
magic was missing this time around, and
worst of all, nobody had much fun making
it, especially not Bill Murray, who felt
like he'd been duped into doing a film
he didn't want to make. The studio was
still interested in doing a third movie,
though, and spent most of the '9s and
even the 2000s trying to get a script
off the grounds. All kinds of ideas were
pitched, but nothing seemed to be
working. And the big sticking point, as
always, was Murray, who was still bitter
about his experiences with Ghostbusters
2 and a major falling out with Harold
Ramos while they were making Groundhog
Day, which drove a wedge between the two
friends that would last almost 20 years.
Jesus, talk about holding a grudge. The
death of Ramos in 2014 basically ended
any chance of a third Ghostbusters
movie, for now at least. But the studio
weren't ready to give up on the
franchise just yet and decided to push
forward with another film for a new
generation. This one would be a reboot,
telling a new origin story for a team of
Ghostbusters and ignoring the continuity
of the original films. And because it
was being remade for modern audiences,
the decision was made to cast an all
female team this time around.
>> You sure about that?
You sure about that?
>> Now, in retrospect, this has been
officially recorded as possibly the
worst decision in Hollywood history. But
it's actually kind of funny to look back
now on a time when an all female reboot
of a classic male franchise was still
this slick cool new idea that nobody had
tried before. Ivan Wrightman was quietly
pushed aside and up and cominging
director Paul Fig was brought in to
replace him. Fig had a solid pedigree of
working in comedy, especially female
ccentric movies, so he was a natural
choice for a project like this. And he
wasted no time bringing in his favorite
actresses Melissa McCarthy and Kristen
Wig for the two lead roles. SNL veterans
Kate McKinnon and Leslie Jones rounded
out the four Ghostbuster roles, and
Chris Hemsworth was somehow convinced to
appear in the movie, too, as Kevin,
their dumb as [ __ ] secretary. I don't
know what his paycheck looked like, but
it must have been [ __ ] good, that's
for sure. Instead of relying on a
tightly written script, Fig's plan was
to make heavy use of improv for most of
the scenes with only a vague outline of
what needed to be accomplished. So,
having comedy actors who could riff off
each other was a big priority for him.
Again, this was another decision that
would soon come back to haunt him big
time. But for now, there were other
problems to contend with. The
announcement of the film being a remake,
the all female cast and FIGA's director
went down about as well as you might
expect online.
[Music]
Fans of the original movies that had
been waiting decades for a third film
were pissed off at the studio for doing
a reboot instead of a continuation. The
decision to gender swap the main
characters was seen as pandering and
exploitative and playing into a culture
war that was just starting to heat up.
And pushing aside Ivan Wrightman in
favor of Paul Fig was seen as
disrespectful to the franchise's legacy.
Damn, it felt like this movie had
nothing going for it. Fee himself didn't
exactly help the controversy by taking
to Twitter and getting combative with
fans every chance he got, which is
always a surefire way to diffuse a tense
situation.
>> Not a great plan. The atmosphere around
this movie was already charged with
controversy before production even
began, and it didn't get much better
once the cameras started rolling in June
2015. According to insiders who
eventually spoke up about their
experiences on set, the problems began
almost immediately. Most of the cast had
signed on without even seeing the script
because they were big fans of
Ghostbusters and genuinely wanted to
make a good movie, which is admirable,
but it's not exactly surprising. I mean,
nobody sets out to make a bad movie
unless their name happens to be Ryan.
But once they actually saw the script
and realized the clown show they were
committed to, the panic set in big time.
The biggest roadblock, both physically
and metaphorically, was Melissa
McCarthy, who was a big fan of the real
Ghostbusters cartoon and expected
something along similar lines. When it
became obvious this movie absolutely
wasn't going to be what she expected,
she began clashing with Fig, leading to
frequent arguments on set. The decision
to rely on improv to get through most of
the scenes wasn't exactly paying
dividends either. Yeah, letting actors
improvise a scene can lead to absolute
gold in the right situation, providing
they're actually good at what they do
and know how to spark off each other.
That absolutely wasn't the case here,
though. And even in the finished cut,
where I assume they cherrypicked the
best of the best, the actors still look
awkward and uncomfortable like they
don't really know what to do next.
Ironically enough, it was Chris
Hemsworth who ended up saving the day.
His scenes are some of the only ones in
the entire movie that actually raised a
smile. And you can only imagine the kind
of crisis this must have triggered for
the main cast when they realized that
Hemsworth, who wasn't even a comedian,
was by far the funniest person on set.
Kristen Wig, on the other hand, was
beginning to realize this movie wasn't
just bad, it had the potential to
seriously [ __ ] up her career. And
looking at her post Ghostbusters
filmography, I think she might have been
on to something. Apparently, she was
wandering around the set in a kind of
shell shocked days and spent most of her
time in her trailer lamenting her
decision to ever get involved in
Ghostbusters one sympathizes.
Old Paul wasn't coping too well with the
pressure either. The climax of the movie
called for a huge song and dance number.
What the [ __ ] is it with this guy in
musical numbers anyway with hundreds of
extras doing a choreographed dance
routine led by Chris Hemsworth?
Unfortunately, it just wasn't coming
together, and Fig ended up completely
melting down about it, crying and
yelling at people. Much like Wig, he was
well aware of how much damage this film
could do to his career if it flopped. He
was very much in over his head, and it
was all becoming too much for him. The
studio were getting pretty nervous as
well, and so they deployed the most
tried and tested weapon in their arsenal
to try and fix it, nostalgia. through a
combination of bribes and threats of
legal action. The original cast were
cajjolled into a series of baffling
cameos playing completely different
characters that had no connection to the
plot. The worst offender was Bill
Murray. You can absolutely tell the guy
doesn't want to be there and delivered
the most phoned in performance of his
career. Well, until then, at least. When
production finally wrapped, it was time
to edit what they had into something
vaguely resembling a movie.
Unfortunately, all that improv was
coming back to haunt Fig again. Not only
was it not particularly funny, but it
led to scenes that meandered on forever,
ballooning the runtime ridiculously
high. The first cut that was screened
for the Sony executives was over 4 hours
long.
I mean, just imagine that for a second.
Imagine having to sit there and watch
four [ __ ] hours of Paul Fig's
Ghostbusters. Naturally, drastic cuts
were ordered, slashing the runtime in
half, but also cutting out a whole bunch
of subplots and character moments and
creating major holes in the story, such
as it was. But if things were bad
already, they were about to get a whole
lot worse when the first trailer dropped
like a thermonuclear bomb. The reaction
from fans was incendury. People
lambasted the lame jokes, the lack of
chemistry in the cast, the oversaturated
colors, the obnoxious soundtrack, and
the baffling attempts to tie into the
original movie that wasn't even part of
this continuity. Worse, the movie was
becoming the focal point in an online
culture war that had been brewing for a
couple of years now and was about to
explode. Panicked re-shoots were quickly
ordered to try to fix the plot holes
caused by the slashed runtime and
address some of the fan criticisms from
the trailer. But Paul and the others
couldn't resist clapping back at the
haters either, which is why we ended up
with scenes like this.
>> And you shouldn't be reading this stuff.
You're not supposed to listen to what
crazy people write in the middle of the
night online.
>> Nice one, Paul. That'll show them.
Ghostbusters finally released in July
2016 to a firestorm of controversy and
backlash. And even though the Access
Media did their best to prop it up with
bought and paid for reviews, there was
no hiding the fact that audiences
absolutely hated it. The movie got weak
audience scores and went on to gross 224
million against 144 million budget,
nowhere near breaking even and killing
any chance of a sequel or, god forbid,
an extended universe.
>> Oh my god.
If I'm ever feeling down about my life,
I console myself with the idea that
somewhere out there in some horrifying
alternate timeline, this movie made like
a billion dollars and there's now like
five or six of the [ __ ] things all
directed by Paul Feige. But luckily for
us, that never came to be. And as the
studio pivoted back towards sequels that
finally got the franchise moving again,
Ghostbusters 2016 was quietly pushed
aside and forgotten, consigned to the
ash heap of cinema history. These days,
its biggest claim to fame is being the
film that kicked off the culture war in
modern entertainment. The first
high-profile example of a film caught up
in identity politics, fan backlash,
studio accusations, and social media
warfare. And the ironic thing is, I
don't think it was really trying to be
any of those things. I think it was the
product of a more innocent and naive
time, a novel idea that nobody took the
time to question properly that stumbled
into an online minefield and triggered
every single one along the way. Paul
Feige probably had no idea what he was
getting into when he signed on for this
movie, and I can't help but wonder if he
would have signed on knowing where it
was all leading. Speaking of which, what
became of old Paul? Well, his fears
about lasting career damage were kind of
proven right. Apart from Hemsworth, who
mostly avoided the backlash, none of the
cast have exactly gone on to bigger and
better things. Paul himself is still
making movies, but mostly in the
low-budget romcom genre, which seems to
be his comfort zone as a filmmaker.
Ghostbusters was his big shot at main
event status. His chance to become one
of the leading creatives in Hollywood.
And for better or worse, he kind of blew
it. But I think his attitude towards
Ghostbusters could neatly be summed up
in this 2017 interview. It's not a
perfect movie. None of my movies are
perfect. I like what we were doing with
it. It was only supposed to be there to
entertain people.
Well, mission not accomplished, my
friend.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
